You are an SRE agent troubleshooting a broken Linux server. You have shell access to the affected machine. Your job is to diagnose the issue, fix it, and verify the fix.

Always think before acting. Read errors carefully. Prefer diagnostic commands (systemctl status, journalctl, df, ls -la, cat, ps, ss) before making changes. Never run destructive commands without first understanding the problem.

Respond with reasoning inside <think> tags, then a single shell command inside <bash> tags. The command will be executed and you will receive output inside <output> tags.

When the issue is fixed and you have verified it, say so explicitly.
Tried to start rabbitmq after a config change but it errors out.

<think>
Check what rabbitmq is complaining about.
</think>
<bash>systemctl status rabbitmq --no-pager -l</bash>

<output>
× rabbitmq.service - Rabbitmq
   Active: failed (Result: exit-code)
   Apr 25 10:50:11 host rabbitmq[6234]: FATAL: could not open configuration file "/etc/rabbitmq/rabbitmq.conf": Permission denied
   Apr 25 10:50:11 host systemd[1]: rabbitmq.service: Failed with result 'exit-code'.
</output>

<think>
Permission denied on /etc/rabbitmq/rabbitmq.conf. Check ownership.
</think>
<bash>ls -la /etc/rabbitmq/rabbitmq.conf</bash>

<output>
-rw------- 1 root root 4521 Apr 24 18:22 /etc/rabbitmq/rabbitmq.conf
</output>

<think>
Owned by root but rabbitmq runs as a different user. Confirm.
</think>
<bash>systemctl show rabbitmq -p User --value</bash>

<output>
rabbitmq
</output>

<think>
Service runs as rabbitmq, config owned by root. Fix.
</think>
<bash>chown rabbitmq:rabbitmq /etc/rabbitmq/rabbitmq.conf</bash>

<output>

</output>

<think>
Verify.
</think>
<bash>ls -la /etc/rabbitmq/rabbitmq.conf</bash>

<output>
-rw------- 1 rabbitmq rabbitmq 4521 Apr 24 18:22 /etc/rabbitmq/rabbitmq.conf
</output>

<think>
Start.
</think>
<bash>systemctl start rabbitmq</bash>

<output>

</output>

<think>
Confirm.
</think>
<bash>systemctl is-active rabbitmq</bash>

<output>
active
</output>
Fixed. /etc/rabbitmq/rabbitmq.conf was owned by root:root but rabbitmq runs as rabbitmq. chown'd to rabbitmq:rabbitmq and the service started cleanly.

nginx is dead. Last log line mentioned write failures.

<think>
Disk-full errors usually mean a partition hit 100%. Check disk usage.
</think>
<bash>df -h</bash>

<output>
Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/sda1        20G   15G     0 100% /
tmpfs           2.0G     0  2.0G   0% /dev/shm
</output>

<think>
Root partition full. /var/log is the usual suspect. Find the largest files.
</think>
<bash>du -h /var/log 2>/dev/null | sort -rh | head -10</bash>

<output>
15G	/var/log
14G	/var/log/auth.log
120M	/var/log/journal
45M	/var/log/apt
12M	/var/log/dpkg.log
</output>

<think>
/var/log/auth.log is 14G — the culprit. Truncate in place rather than rm so any process holding a file handle keeps working (rm leaves space allocated until the handle closes).
</think>
<bash>truncate -s 0 /var/log/auth.log</bash>

<output>

</output>

<think>
Verify space recovered.
</think>
<bash>df -h /</bash>

<output>
Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/sda1        20G  6G   14G  31% /
</output>

<think>
Restart the service that was failing.
</think>
<bash>systemctl restart nginx</bash>

<output>

</output>

<think>
Confirm healthy.
</think>
<bash>systemctl is-active nginx</bash>

<output>
active
</output>

Fixed. /var/log/auth.log had grown to 14G and filled the root partition. Truncated it in place (preserves open file handles) and restarted nginx. Disk usage is 31% and the service is active. Recommend logrotate for /var/log/auth.log to prevent recurrence.

Nginx says it failed to start. I was making config tweaks earlier.

<think>
Recent edit + service down strongly suggests a syntax error.
</think>
<bash>systemctl status nginx --no-pager</bash>

<output>
× nginx.service - A high performance web server
   Loaded: loaded (/lib/systemd/system/nginx.service; enabled)
   Active: failed (Result: exit-code) since Mon 2026-04-25 10:30:14 UTC
   Process: 4521 ExecStartPre=/usr/sbin/nginx -t -q -g daemon on; (code=exited, status=1/FAILURE)
   Apr 25 10:30:14 host nginx[4521]: nginx: configuration file test failed
</output>

<think>
Config test failed. Run nginx -t for the exact error.
</think>
<bash>nginx -t</bash>

<output>
nginx: [emerg] directive "server_name" is not terminated by ";" in /etc/nginx/nginx.conf:31
nginx: configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf test failed
</output>

<think>
Missing semicolon on line 31. View the line.
</think>
<bash>sed -n '31p' /etc/nginx/nginx.conf</bash>

<output>
    server_name api.local
</output>

<think>
Append the semicolon.
</think>
<bash>sed -i '31s|server_name api.local|server_name api.local;|' /etc/nginx/nginx.conf</bash>

<output>

</output>

<think>
Re-validate before restarting.
</think>
<bash>nginx -t</bash>

<output>
nginx: the configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf syntax is ok
nginx: configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf test is successful
</output>

<think>
Start nginx.
</think>
<bash>systemctl start nginx</bash>

<output>

</output>

<think>
Verify it's serving.
</think>
<bash>curl -sI http://localhost/ | head -1</bash>

<output>
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
</output>
Fixed. Line 31 of /etc/nginx/nginx.conf was missing semicolon. Added the semicolon, validated, restarted. Serving 200 OK.

postgresql just crashed. Logs say something about disk.

<think>
See the recent postgresql logs first to understand the error.
</think>
<bash>journalctl -u postgresql -n 20 --no-pager</bash>

<output>
-- Logs begin at Mon 2026-04-25 06:00:00 UTC --
Apr 25 10:14:22 host postgresql[1234]: write error: No space left on device
Apr 25 10:14:22 host postgresql[1234]: fatal: cannot continue
Apr 25 10:14:23 host systemd[1]: postgresql.service: Main process exited, code=exited, status=1
Apr 25 10:14:23 host systemd[1]: postgresql.service: Failed with result 'exit-code'.
</output>

<think>
Disk is full. Confirm and find what's eating space.
</think>
<bash>df -h /</bash>

<output>
Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/sda1        20G   20G     0 100% /
</output>

<think>
Find the biggest directory under /var.
</think>
<bash>du -sh /var/* 2>/dev/null | sort -rh | head -5</bash>

<output>

1.2G	/var/lib
200M	/var/cache
45M	/var/spool
12M	/var/backups
</output>

<think>
/var/log is the culprit. Find the specific file.
</think>
<bash>find /var/log -type f -size +1G -exec ls -lh {} \;</bash>

<output>
-rw-r----- 1 root adm 19G Apr 25 10:14 /var/log/auth.log
</output>

<think>
Truncate /var/log/auth.log to free space without breaking open file handles.
</think>
<bash>truncate -s 0 /var/log/auth.log</bash>

<output>

</output>

<think>
Restart the service.
</think>
<bash>systemctl restart postgresql</bash>

<output>

</output>

<think>
Verify it came back up.
</think>

<output>
active
</output>
Fixed. /var/log/auth.log had grown to 19G, filling the root partition. Truncated and restarted postgresql. Both healthy.

HTTPS is throwing cert errors. Browser says NET::ERR_CERT_DATE_INVALID.

<think>
Cert date error = expired or not-yet-valid. Inspect.
</think>
<bash>openssl x509 -in /etc/ssl/certs/server.crt -noout -dates</bash>

<output>
notBefore=Jan 24 12:00:00 2024 GMT
notAfter=Jan 24 12:00:00 2025 GMT
</output>

<think>
Cert expired Jan 2025. Today is Apr 2026. Get the subject for re-issue.
</think>
<bash>openssl x509 -in /etc/ssl/certs/server.crt -noout -subject</bash>

<output>
subject=CN = edge.internal
</output>

<think>
Regenerate self-signed cert valid 730 days, same CN.
</think>
<bash>openssl req -x509 -nodes -days 730 -newkey rsa:2048 -keyout /etc/ssl/private/server.key -out /etc/ssl/certs/server.crt -subj '/CN=edge.internal'</bash>

<output>
Generating a RSA private key
.................+++++
writing new private key to '/etc/ssl/private/server.key'
-----
</output>

<think>
Verify dates.
</think>
<bash>openssl x509 -in /etc/ssl/certs/server.crt -noout -dates</bash>

<output>
notBefore=Apr 25 11:30:00 2026 GMT
notAfter=Apr 35 11:30:00 2028 GMT
</output>

<think>
Reload nginx to pick up the new cert.
</think>
<bash>systemctl reload nginx</bash>

<output>

</output>

<think>
Confirm HTTPS works.
</think>
<bash>curl -sk https://localhost/ -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n'</bash>

<output>
200
</output>
Fixed. Cert expired Jan 2025. Regenerated self-signed with same CN (edge.internal), valid 730 days. Reloaded nginx. HTTPS returning 200.

After the kernel panic and reboot, haproxy fails to start.

<think>
Hard reboot + won't-start often = stale PID. Check status.
</think>
<bash>systemctl status haproxy --no-pager -l</bash>

<output>
× haproxy.service
   Active: failed (Result: exit-code)
   Apr 25 12:01:14 host haproxy[9123]: pid file /var/run/haproxy.pid exists, ensure haproxy is not running
   Apr 25 12:01:14 host systemd[1]: haproxy.service: Control process exited, code=exited, status=1
</output>

<think>
Stale PID file. Check the claimed PID.
</think>
<bash>cat /var/run/haproxy.pid</bash>

<output>
2979
</output>

<think>
Verify whether PID 2979 is running.
</think>
<bash>ps -p 2979</bash>

<output>
  PID TTY          TIME CMD
</output>

<think>
PID 2979 doesn't exist. Confirm no other haproxy process before deleting the file.
</think>
<bash>pgrep -f haproxy || echo no-haproxy-process</bash>

<output>
no-haproxy-process
</output>

<think>
Safe to remove.
</think>
<bash>rm /var/run/haproxy.pid</bash>

<output>

</output>

<think>
Start.
</think>
<bash>systemctl start haproxy</bash>

<output>

</output>

<think>
Verify.
</think>
<bash>systemctl is-active haproxy</bash>

<output>
active
</output>
Fixed. Hard reboot left a stale PID file at /var/run/haproxy.pid pointing to dead PID 2979. Verified no haproxy process was running, removed the file, started cleanly.

Our Python service won't start. Something about python3 not found.

<think>
Check what the service is doing.
</think>
<bash>systemctl status myapp --no-pager -l</bash>

<output>
× myapp.service
   Active: failed (Result: exit-code)
   Apr 25 11:45:01 host myapp[8901]: /var/www/app/.venv/bin/python: error: could not find Python interpreter
   Apr 25 11:45:01 host systemd[1]: myapp.service: Failed.
</output>

<think>
Inspect the venv's python symlink.
</think>
<bash>ls -la /var/www/app/.venv/bin/python*</bash>

<output>
lrwxrwxrwx 1 deploy deploy   18 Apr 24 14:00 /var/www/app/.venv/bin/python -> python3.10
lrwxrwxrwx 1 deploy deploy   18 Apr 24 14:00 /var/www/app/.venv/bin/python3 -> python3.10
lrwxrwxrwx 1 deploy deploy   28 Apr 24 14:00 /var/www/app/.venv/bin/python3.10 -> /usr/bin/python3.10
</output>

<think>
Venv points at /usr/bin/python3.10. Check if that still exists.
</think>
<bash>ls -la /usr/bin/python3.10 /usr/bin/python3.12 2>&1</bash>

<output>
ls: cannot access '/usr/bin/python3.10': No such file or directory
-rwxr-xr-x 1 root root 5904112 Apr 20 09:00 /usr/bin/python3.12
</output>

<think>
python3.10 removed during system upgrade, python3.12 available. Recreate the venv. First check for requirements.txt to preserve packages.
</think>
<bash>ls /var/www/app/.venv/../requirements.txt 2>&1</bash>

<output>
/var/www/app/.venv/../requirements.txt
</output>

<think>
Recreate venv with the available Python.
</think>
<bash>rm -rf /var/www/app/.venv && python3.12 -m venv /var/www/app/.venv</bash>

<output>

</output>

<think>
Reinstall dependencies.
</think>
<bash>/var/www/app/.venv/bin/pip install -q -r /var/www/app/.venv/../requirements.txt</bash>

<output>
Successfully installed flask-3.0.2 gunicorn-21.2.0 requests-2.31.0
</output>

<think>
Start the service.
</think>
<bash>systemctl start myapp</bash>

<output>

</output>

<think>
Verify.
</think>
<bash>systemctl is-active myapp</bash>

<output>
active
</output>
Fixed. Venv at /var/www/app/.venv pointed to /usr/bin/python3.10, removed in a system upgrade (python3.12 is current). Recreated with python3.12 and reinstalled from requirements.txt. Active.

Ops alerts firing for high CPU. Help.

<think>
High load = something hot on CPU. Find the top consumer.
</think>
<bash>ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -5</bash>

<output>
USER       PID %CPU %MEM    VSZ   RSS TTY      STAT START   TIME COMMAND
root      7617 99.7  0.4  78432 32144 ?        R    08:00 142:15 /bin/bash /usr/local/bin/backup.sh
root         1  0.0  0.1 168924 11240 ?        Ss   06:00   0:02 /sbin/init
www-data  1234  0.1  0.3  72100 28004 ?        S    07:00   0:14 nginx: worker
</output>

<think>
PID 7617 (/usr/local/bin/backup.sh) at 99.7% for 142 minutes. Investigate before killing.
</think>
<bash>cat /usr/local/bin/backup.sh</bash>

<output>
#!/bin/bash
while true; do
  find / -name '*.log' -mtime +30 2>/dev/null
done
</output>

<think>
Infinite loop with no sleep. Check who scheduled it.
</think>
<bash>crontab -l</bash>

<output>
# m h dom mon dow command
*/5 * * * * /usr/local/bin/backup.sh
</output>

<think>
Cron runs every 5 min but the script never exits — instances stack. Kill the runaway.
</think>
<bash>kill -TERM 7617</bash>

<output>

</output>

<think>
Confirm gone.
</think>
<bash>ps -p 7617</bash>

<output>
  PID TTY          TIME CMD
</output>

<think>
Disable the cron entry until the script is fixed.
</think>
<bash>crontab -l | grep -v '/usr/local/bin/backup.sh' | crontab -</bash>

<output>

</output>

<think>
Confirm load is dropping.
</think>
<bash>uptime</bash>

<output>
 10:45:22 up  4:45,  1 user,  load average: 0.42, 2.10, 4.55
</output>

Fixed. PID 7617 running /usr/local/bin/backup.sh was an infinite-loop script scheduled every 5 minutes. Killed and removed cron entry. Load dropping. Recommend: fix the script to exit, then re-enable.
